text: Welcome to this video from the James Cook University Hospital. I'm going to show you a left lower lobe sleeve resection with subxiphoid removal.
The surgeon inserts robotic arm ports, including a subxiphoid port, using scissors to gently push the tissue over the pericardium to facilitate port placement.

The surgeon has placed four robotic ports and an additional utility incision for the assistant. A Maryland bipolar forceps is visible in the right robotic arm, while a ProGrasp instrument is in the left robotic arm. The setup is prepared for a lower lobectomy with subxiphoid sample removal.
which is a much softer touch and much easier to hold the lung, but it had actually expired, so I had to use Progras.
The surgeon exposes the pulmonary artery (PA) and identifies the inferior ligament on the left-hand side. A station L-line lymph node is visible, which the surgeon plans to excise.
The surgeon has freed the inferior pulmonary vein and is retracting the lung using a lung grasper through a five-millimeter port.
The surgeon retracts the lung forward using a lung grasper and releases the pleura posteriorly. Station seven lymph node is removed, located just anterior to the vagus nerve.

The surgical field displays a clear view of the inferior pulmonary ligament and inferior pulmonary vein. Station seven lymph nodes are being removed, with the left main bronchus visible just above these structures.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to clean the area around the pulmonary artery, removing a station L-ten lymph node for better visibility. The bronchus is visible adjacent to the pulmonary artery.
The surgeon identifies the left main bronchus, lower lobe bronchus, upper lobe bronchus, and the secondary carina. The view shifts anteriorly to reveal the superior pulmonary vein.
The surgeon identifies the lingular vein, noting its lower-than-normal position. The inferior pulmonary vein and superior pulmonary vein are visible, with a distinct gap between them.
The surgeon begins transecting the inferior pulmonary vein using a surgical stapler with tan cartridges, followed by the transection of the pulmonary artery.

Just a note on this patient, quite an interesting patient. They had a bronchoscopy for middle lobe collapse. The middle lobe was normal, but they looked down the left lower lobe and found a very small abnormality between the apical and basal segment of the left lower lobe.

They biopsied this, and it came back as carcinoma in situ. This was biopsied a second time, which confirmed still carcinoma in situ, very close to the lower lobe take-off. We did further autofluorescence to confirm there was no carcinoma in situ in either the upper lobe or the left main bronchus. And then we planned to perform a sleeve lobectomy to get the maximum chance of full resection with clear margins.

The surgeon uses a TAMBODY-Five stapler to transect the basal branch of the pulmonary artery. A lung grasp is employed to maneuver around the artery due to limited reach, facilitating the successful completion of the transection.

The surgeon efficiently staples all basal pulmonary artery branches, including the apical branch of the lower lobe, in a single firing. The lingular branch of the pulmonary artery is pointed out, emphasizing the need to preserve it without causing stenosis. The anterior fissure is then transected after the vein and artery have been taken.

The surgeon cleans and exposes the left main bronchus, identifying the lower lobe bronchus inferiorly and the upper lobe bronchus to the left. The planned incisions around these bronchial structures are shown.
The surgeon switches to curved scissors and begins making an incision at the crux between the lower lobe and the upper lobe, precisely at the carina.
The surgeon proceeds in an angled direction, making an incision through the secondary carina, a thick piece of tissue, and continues towards the upper lobe bronchus.

The surgeon opens the bronchus, revealing the left main bronchus in the distance. A bronchoscope is used for internal visualization, with light visible from the bronchoscope. The double lumen tube is also visible. The view includes the lower lobe bronchus.

The surgeon adjusts the lower lobe to enhance visibility for completing the resection. A V-shaped incision is made in the lower lobe bronchus, rather than a straight sleeve, due to the tumor's location. The incisions are joined horizontally, and the membranous part of the bronchus is cut.

The surgeon visually identifies the pulmonary artery above and the aorta to the right. Below the scissors, the superior vein and lingular vein are visible, positioned slightly lower than usual. The view into the lower lobe bronchus reveals an abnormality at the carina between the apical and basal systems.

And the sleeve is due to concerns that carcinoma in situ can crawl along bronchi a little. I certainly didn't want to staple across that. And if you're not going to staple, you're going to cut, you might as well do a formal sleeve.

The surgeon is fashioning the upper lobe bronchus, straightening it at a slight angle to improve resection margins. The upper lobe bronchus and the left main bronchus are visible, prepared for anastomosis.
The surgeon demonstrates the use of a Foro V-Lock Suture, highlighting its absorbable monofilament design with visible one-way barbs that maintain tension during the sleeve procedure.

The surgeon uses a V-Lock suture with a small eye at the end, threading the suture through the eye and pulling it tight to secure tension without needing a knot. The suture is visibly tightened, demonstrating its ability to maintain tension efficiently.

The surgeon begins a continuous suture along the cartilaginous portion of the bronchus, moving from superior to inferior. The suture visibly tightens the tissue, preparing for a second suture along the membranous portion.

The surgeon begins continuous suturing using a V-lock suture, demonstrating its ability to maintain tension without loosening. The robotic system provides enhanced vision with eight times magnification, allowing precise visualization of the suturing process. The surgeon occasionally takes both bites in one motion, tightening the suture effectively.

The surgeon holds a suture cap needle driver in the right hand through port one, demonstrating its use for handling the V-lock suture. The needle driver includes scissors proximally, which can be used for cutting the suture, although there is a risk of accidentally cutting the suture itself.
The surgeon performs the next continuous suture, carefully tightening it to maintain tension. The suture is completed in two steps, ensuring it remains secure.

The surgeon uses robotic assistance to tighten the continuous suture on the pulmonary tissue, utilizing the enhanced vision to efficiently take suture bites in one motion. The one-way suture is pulled tight, ensuring it remains secure without loosening.

You can never accidentally loosen yourself if you want to tighten the sutures, which is yet another advantage of V-Lock. V-Lock comes in a variety of sizes. This is IV-O, it also comes in III-O, and a wide other variety.
The surgeon uses a large needle, size four O, to perform single bites during the bronchial anastomosis, demonstrating its suitability for this part of the procedure.
The surgeon navigates around the cartilaginous portion of the bronchus, using an endoscopic technique to perform a continuous suture for the bronchial anastomosis.
The surgeon performs continuous suturing, taking larger bites on the left main bronchus compared to the left upper lobe bronchus, with the sutures visibly aligning well.

The surgeon carefully aligns the continuous suture along the bronchial anastomosis, ensuring precise pairing at each stage. The upper lobe vein is visible near the suture line, with clear visualization preventing accidental needle contact.

although lobectomies have been performed robotically from all lobes. We've also performed a total lung-sparing sleeve using this exact same view, and it was very straightforward. And I've also watched Bob Sofoglio do a left-sided total lung-sparing sleeve with no problems at all.

So one little tip that I should give myself is we're running out of suture. This suture is fifteen centimetres which I think is quite long but actually it's quite important not to run out of suture. I probably did one suture too many as it was just a little bit tricky to tie at the end. But anyway, here's my last suture here, and I'll now change needle and go and start at the membranous portion.
The surgeon uses a V-lock suture to secure the upper border of the membranous portion, ensuring it remains taut while preparing to start the next suture line.

The surgeon uses the Prograsp instrument throughout the procedure, although a second needle driver could be used. The Prograsp is noted to be strong, and the preferred Cadillac instrument was unavailable due to expiration.

And the Progras is the very strongest grip. They look the same as a Cadillac or a bipolar. But as you're about to see, the Progras grips so strongly, it can be dangerous. And certainly in lung surgery, if you grasp the lung with the Progras, you can rip the lung. And I'm just grabbing the suture with the Progras right now. And it's actually just snapped the suture just by grasping with a pergrass. It's that strong. So that's just to be aware of if you're new to robotics. They look the same, but they certainly don't grip the same.

So I've had to take a new suture and just repeat this initial first row. Then we go outside to in, then inside to out, and then we use the little eye that the V-lock has instead of a first throw. I've shortly got a VATS sleeve coming up and I think I'm definitely going to use V-lock by VATS and I'd probably be quite tempted to use a V-lock in open surgery as well for those not suitable for an endoscopic route just because it really is so good at keeping its tension really nicely workable and saves you time and I don't know if I'm speaking out of turn but I think it's not that expensive either

The surgeon carefully sutures the delicate membranous portion of the upper lobe bronchus, ensuring not to tear the tissue while performing a continuous suture along this section before aligning with the other suture.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the needle with a curved approach to suture the thin membranous portion of the bronchus, ensuring not to tear the delicate tissue. The suture is tightened gently using a V-lock, avoiding excessive tension.
The Progras instrument is holding a thick section of the apical bronchus, likely at the secondary carina. The surgeon considers cutting just above this area.

The surgeon uses the ProGrass instrument to pull and tighten the suture, pairing the upper lobe with the left main bronchus. The cut is made just above the secondary carina, avoiding the thick tissue area.
The surgeon completes the final sutures on the membranous portion of the upper lobe bronchus, using the ProGrass instrument to apply tension and secure the sutures.

The surgeon examines the end of the needle and follows the suture back, revealing a break in the suture. This break is attributed to excessive force applied by the ProGrasp instrument. The surgeon advises against holding the V-lock suture with another needle driver, recommending the use of soft grasping forceps like caviar or bipolar instead.

The surgeon places a third V-lock suture, securing it into its eyelet, and prepares to hand-tie a knot between the snap suture and the newly placed V-lock. The Prograsp instrument is used to ensure the suture remains secure.

Now there are some really good videos on YouTube on left lower lobe sleeves by VATS, but certainly in my own hands as an anterior approach VATS lobectomist, I would find this extremely difficult technically. I just don't think we've got the VATS instruments that have got the dexterity to suture in this way easily. And I'm not saying it's not possible. I'll just say this is where the power of the robot comes in in thoracic surgery. It's suturing because it was originally designed as a suturing device, and that's its real strength.
The surgeon uses a robotic system to ensure the first suture in the bronchial anastomosis is not overlapped, demonstrating the precision of robotic suturing in a pulmonary lobectomy.

The surgeon cuts the V-lock suture by pulling it intentionally, then ties the final knot, connecting the cartilaginous suture to the membranous suture with multiple throws, demonstrating the ease of this technique.
The surgeon uses a needle driver to carefully tighten the knot, ensuring it is not too tight to avoid snapping. The robotic system provides visual feedback during this process.
The bronchoscopic view shows the final anastomosis, which appears widely open. The lingula is visible but not fully open, as the lung has not been inflated yet.

The surgeon performs an underwater check of the bronchial anastomosis, observing a few small bubbles originating from the lung parenchyma. The water is suctioned below the parenchyma to confirm that no bubbles are coming from the anastomosis site.

The surgeon removes the tumor through a sub-xiphoid incision, noting that the circular opening of the tissues facilitates easier extraction with minimal rib spreading. The linea alba is cut, which is visually less invasive and allows for a smaller incision.

The surgeon checks the wide open margin of the resected area, with the lower lobe bronchus visible. A drain is placed, and the lung is inflated. The video concludes with the post-operative status showing a fully inflated lung without bubbling, leading to the removal of the drain.
The final result of the pulmonary lobectomy is shown with five visible incisions and the sub-xiphoid removal site.
